items.q: Why do Jews not accept Jesus as the Messiah?
items.correct: Because the Jewish Messiah is expected to bring peace, rebuild the Temple and restore Israel — events the Jewish tradition holds Jesus did not accomplish.
items.misconception: Because Jews believe Jesus never existed.
items.random_wrong: Because Jesus was crucified, and the Messiah cannot die.
items.why: Most Jewish thinkers accept Jesus existed as a historical figure. The disagreement is about whether he fulfilled messianic prophecies — a theological judgement, not denial of history.

items.q: What does the Buddhist doctrine of anatta teach?
items.correct: There is no permanent, unchanging self or soul — what we call 'self' is a constantly changing flow of five aggregates (skandhas). This is distinctively Buddhist and differs from the Hindu atman.
items.misconception: Buddhists believe in a soul like the Christian or Hindu version.
items.random_wrong: Anatta means Buddhists have no personality.
items.why: Anatta is central to Buddhism and explicitly rejects the Hindu atman doctrine. Students assume all Eastern religions share the same soul concept, missing the deliberate contrast Buddhism draws.

items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: Which Catholic belief is that bread and wine become Christ's body and blood?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Transubstantiation. “Consubstantiation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which year did Mary appear at Lourdes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1858. “1517” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many days does Lent last (not counting Sundays in many traditions)?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
